context_block_lines: - attribution: Inspired by firecrawl/open-lovable (MIT). See LICENSES/open-lovable-mit.txt.
1. **Never copy bytes from the source.** The L3 transformer already
replaced every source image with a placeholder record. You MUST
NOT request a network fetch of any URL listed under
`source_url` of an image record — that field is provenance-only,
pinned in the W11.7 manifest for audit replay.
emit must point at the placeholder URL provided in the image
3. **No source brand names in user-visible copy.** The L3 rewrite
already paraphrased the source — preserve that paraphrase.
Never regress to the original wording even if you can guess
4. **Attribution travels with the artefact.** The W11.7 traceability
pinned by the framework adapter; your scaffolding must not
5. **Echo the manifest fingerprint.** When you produce a project
README, a header comment, or a metadata file, include the
`clone_id` and `manifest_hash` shown above so the W11.12 audit
## Rewritten outline (design inspiration only — already paraphrased by L3)
- title: Our take: Why we rewrote our build system — Our Brand Engineering Blog
heading: A page about: Why we rewrote our build system

tagline: After three years of escalating Makefile pain, we finally bit the bullet and migrated the whole engineering organisation onto a proper task graph runner. Here is what we learned, what we shipped, and what we would do differently.

- **The pain we were running from** — Our old build pipeline was a 4,000-line Makefile with implicit rules layered on top of explicit ones. Onboarding a new engineer took three weeks. A typo in one rule could silently rebuild every artefact. We could not parallelise across mac…

- **What we shipped** — We picked a content-addressed task graph runner with first-class remote caching. The migration took six engineers four months — about half the time we estimated, mostly because we wrote a small DSL that translated existing Make targets int…

- **What surprised us** — The biggest unexpected win was that the new system surfaced flaky tests we did not know we had. The old Makefile retried silently; the new runner failed loudly. We fixed eighty-seven flakes in the first month.

- **What we would change** — We would migrate test targets first, application targets second, and infrastructure targets last. We did the opposite, and infrastructure became a long tail that blocked the cutover for two months.
- footer: Written by the Our Brand Engineering team — © 2026 Our Brand. RSS Contact

sections.heading: The pain we were running from
sections.summary: Our old build pipeline was a 4,000-line Makefile with implicit rules layered on top of explicit ones. Onboarding a new engineer took three weeks. A typo in one rule could silently rebuild every artefact. We could not parallelise across machines because the dependency graph was n…

sections.heading: What we shipped
sections.summary: We picked a content-addressed task graph runner with first-class remote caching. The migration took six engineers four months — about half the time we estimated, mostly because we wrote a small DSL that translated existing Make targets into the new format.

sections.heading: What surprised us
sections.summary: The biggest unexpected win was that the new system surfaced flaky tests we did not know we had. The old Makefile retried silently; the new runner failed loudly. We fixed eighty-seven flakes in the first month.

sections.heading: What we would change
sections.summary: We would migrate test targets first, application targets second, and infrastructure targets last. We did the opposite, and infrastructure became a long tail that blocked the cutover for two months.
